response: The internet traces its roots to 1960s U.S. Defense Department networking research, with ARPANET’s first UCLA-to-Stanford message in 1969 and a switch to TCP/IP in 1983 establishing its modern architecture. Tim Berners-Lee’s 1989 invention of the World Wide Web (HTML, URLs, HTTP) and the Mosaic browser in 1993 opened the system to the public, fueling the late-1990s dot-com boom—whose 2001 crash wiped out many firms but left giants like Amazon and Google standing. Social media platforms such as Facebook, Twitter, and YouTube (mid-2000s) and the iPhone-driven mobile era (from 2007), along with cloud services like AWS (launched 2006), further reshaped usage and business models. Today the internet serves more than 5 billion people worldwide and underpins commerce, communication, and entertainment across nearly every sector of life.
